coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescent and visual determination of ibandronic acid using a carbon dots/AgInS2 quantum dots ratiometric sensing platform
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Castro, Rafael C.
Páscoa, Ricardo N.M.J.
Saraiva, M. Lúcia M.F.S.
Santos, João L.M.
Ribeiro, David S.M.
coredata.dc:description: A sensing platform combining carbon dots (CDs, with blue emission) and thiomalic acid (TMA)-capped AgInS2 quantum dots (QDs, with orange emission) was developed aiming the photoluminescence (PL) ratiometric determination of ibandronic acid (IBAN), a bisphosphonate pharmaceutical. The ternary AgInS2 QDs were used for IBAN probing, undergoing a concentration-related PL quenching in its presence, whilst the PL of CDs remained practically unaffected due to its chemical inertness towards the antiresorptive drug, provided an intrinsic self-reference fluorophore. In addition, a visual sensing approach was also proposed, employing for the first time ternary QDs. This relied on RGB images acquired by means of a digital camera and seek the development of a rapid IBAN screening test. The developed sensing platforms were employed for IBAN determination in samples with pharmaceutical interest providing good results, in accordance to the reported IBAN levels, and obtaining recovery values between 98 and 103%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric sensing platform
AgInS2 quantum dots
Carbon dots
Ibandronic acid
Photoluminescence
Visual detection

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: AIS QDs and CDs synthesis procedure
Procedure for the visual detection approach
Preliminary assays for conventional fluorometric assay
CDs/AIS-QDs ratiometric sensing platform for IBAN detection
Sensing platform for visual detection of IBAN
Optimization study for the development of the CDs/AIS QDs arrangement
Stability study of the visual sensing probes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Rafael C. Castro thanks FCT (Funda??o para a Ci?ncia e Tecnologia) for his PhD grant ref. 2020.08465.BD. Ricardo N. M. J. P?scoa and David S. M. Ribeiro thank FCT (Funda??o para a Ci?ncia e Tecnologia) for funding through program DL 57/2016 ? Norma transit?ria. This work received financial support from PT national funds (FCT/MCTES, Funda??o para a Ci?ncia e Tecnologia and Minist?rio da Ci?ncia, Tecnologia e Ensino Superior) through Grant UIDB/QUI/50006/2020. Financing from Programa INTERREG V A Espanha Portugal (POCTEP) under the project FOODSENS and from the European Union (FEDER funds through the Operational Competitiveness Program (COMPETE) POCI-01-0145-FEDER-030163? Project Tailored NanoGUMBOS: The green key to wound infections chemsensing) were also greatly appreciated.

Rafael C. Castro thanks FCT (Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia) for his PhD grant ref. 2020.08465.BD. Ricardo N. M. J. Páscoa and David S. M. Ribeiro thank FCT (Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia) for funding through program DL 57/2016 – Norma transitória. This work received financial support from PT national funds (FCT/MCTES, Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia and Ministério da Ciência, Tecnologia e Ensino Superior) through Grant UIDB/QUI/50006/2020. Financing from Programa INTERREG V A Espanha Portugal (POCTEP) under the project FOODSENS and from the European Union (FEDER funds through the Operational Competitiveness Program (COMPETE) POCI-01-0145-FEDER-030163– Project Tailored NanoGUMBOS: The green key to wound infections chemsensing) were also greatly appreciated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Normalized PL spectra of a) TMA-AIS, b) MPA-AIS, c) D-Pen-AIS QDs and d) CDs upon excitation at 360 nm. Dashed and full line correspond to the PL spectra of semiconductors QDs after and before lyophilization process.
Normalized PL spectra of nanoparticles sensing platforms (λex = 360 nm) by adding ibandronic acid with increasing concentrations. a) TMA-AIS QDs; b) MPA-AIS QDs; c) D-Pen-AIS QDs; d) CDs.

PL spectra (λex = 360 nm) of the sensing platform during 30 min alone (a) and by adding 16.60 µmol L−1 of IBAN (c). Ratio of the emission intensities of the 430/583 nm sensing platform as a function of time alone (b) and by adding 16.60 µmol L−1 of IBAN (d).

a) PL spectra of ratiometric probe when interacting with increasing concentrations of IBAN (λex = 360 nm). b) The obtained calibration curve between the emission intensities ratios (FICD/FIAIS) and bisphosphonate concentration (R2 = 0.9928, n = 11).

Fluorescence images of all tested nanomaterials at different concentration levels (different dilution factors from the crude solutions, DF) and the correspondent emission spectra. A 365 nm UV lamp was employed to photograph all the solutions.

Fluorescence images of visual ratiometric probe by combining CDs and yellow (a), orange (b), and red (c) TMA-AIS QDs, respectively, at different concentration levels. For a fixed concentration level of CDs (1:4000 from crude solution), different dilution factors of each ternary QDs from crude solution were mixed. A 365 nm UV lamp was employed to photograph all the solutions.

a) PL images of visual sensing probe when interacting with increasing concentrations of IBAN and samples; b) The obtained calibration curves between the blue and red channels intensities ratio versus IBAN concentration.
Maximum emission wavelength and quantum yield values of each as-synthetized nanomaterial solutions and after the lyophilization process.
PL lifetime values of each synthesized ternary AIS QDs and CDs. τi correspond to the PL lifetimes of each decay fraction and Bi are their corresponding fractional intensities.
IBAN monitoring in pharmaceutical formulations and the obtained recovery values in spiked samples by applying the proposed PL ratiometric approach.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescent and visual determination of ibandronic acid using a carbon dots/AgInS2 quantum dots ratiometric sensing platform

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: LAQV, REQUIMTE, Department of Chemical Sciences, Laboratory of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Porto, Rua de Jorge Viterbo Ferreira n° 228, 4050-313 Porto, Portugal

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Rua de Jorge Viterbo Ferreira n° 228

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: LAQV, REQUIMTE, Department of Chemical Sciences, Laboratory of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, University of Porto, Rua de Jorge Viterbo Ferreira n° 228, 4050-313 Porto, Portugal

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensing platform combining carbon dots (CDs, with blue emission) and thiomalic acid (TMA)-capped AgInS2 quantum dots (QDs, with orange emission) was developed aiming the photoluminescence (PL) ratiometric determination of ibandronic acid (IBAN), a bisphosphonate pharmaceutical. The ternary AgInS2 QDs were used for IBAN probing, undergoing a concentration-related PL quenching in its presence, whilst the PL of CDs remained practically unaffected due to its chemical inertness towards the antiresorptive drug, provided an intrinsic self-reference fluorophore. In addition, a visual sensing approach was also proposed, employing for the first time ternary QDs. This relied on RGB images acquired by means of a digital camera and seek the development of a rapid IBAN screening test. The developed sensing platforms were employed for IBAN determination in samples with pharmaceutical interest providing good results, in accordance to the reported IBAN levels, and obtaining recovery values between 98 and 103%.

ce:section.ce:para: Bisphosphonates belongs to a class of active pharmacological compounds that act as antiresorptive agents and inhibitors of osteoclast activity, which result in a progressive gain in bone mass and consequently a decrease in the risk of fractures [1,2]. For this reason, these compounds are widely used in the treatment of skeletal disorders which include not only osteoporosis but also Paget’s disease and bone metastasis [3]. In addition, this pharmacological class of compounds are relevant for prevention and treatment of cancer due to their direct antiproliferative effects, preventing tumor cell adhesion to the bone matrix [4,5].

Bisphosphonates are classified as non-nitrogenous and nitrogenous compounds wherein the last ones exhibit 100 to 10000-fold higher antiresorptive potency relatively to etidronate [6]. One of the most widely used and efficient nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate is ibandronic acid (IBAN), which is a third-generation bisphosphonate containing a tertiary amine in its chemical structure [2]. In contrast to other bisphosphonates drugs commonly analyzed by UV–Vis spectrophotometry or fluorometry, the determination of IBAN by one of these analytical techniques is an interesting challenge for the scientific community due to its characteristic tertiary amine moiety that hampered the formation of chromophore derivatives [7]. On account of the intrinsic characteristics of IBAN, such as the absence of fluorophores and/or chromophores, low volatility and high polarity, the number of analytical methodologies disclosed in the scientific literature for its monitoring is scarce. The majority of the methodologies for IBAN determination includes chromatographic techniques with conductometric [8], mass spectrophotometric [9], evaporative light-scattering [10], indirect fluorometric [3] and indirect photometric [11,12] detection. The abovementioned methodologies are reliable, robust, accurate and precise, however they are time-consuming, requiring specialized technicians and appropriate laboratory conditions containing complex instrumentation.

Therefore, the continuous search for developing simpler and faster alternatives for IBAN determination in real samples, while maintaining well-suited analytical figures of merit, is an enticing challenge for scientists. On this context, fluorometric methodologies exhibit the sort of features capable of fulfilling these purposes. Accordingly, and given the impossibility of directly determining IBAN due to the absence of native fluorescence, the use of photoluminescent (PL) quantum dots (QDs) as sensing probes can be anticipated as a valuable alternative [13]. It should be mentioned that an unique QDs-based PL sensing approach for IBAN determination has been proposed hitherto [14]. In the referred work, CdTe QDs stabilized with mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) were used as sensing elements. Despite their remarkable optical features that make them efficient PL probes, they exhibit some drawbacks specifically related with the intrinsic toxicity of Cd. Therefore, semiconductors QDs composed by multicomponents in ternary arrangements and containing less toxic and more abundant elements, namely AgInS2 (AIS) and CuInS2 (CIS), have emerged as a valuable option regarding binary QDs like CdTe or CdSe nanocrystals [15-19]. Exhibiting interesting intrinsic characteristics, such as extended emission wavelength range, high attenuation coefficients and a relatively large Stokes shift, Cd-free AIS and CIS QDs have been recently used for the development of environmentally friendly analytical methodologies involving distinct sensing mechanisms namely, photoluminescence (PL) modulation [15,16,20,21] or QDs-enhanced chemiluminescence (CL) [17,22]. Another alternative to the use of Cd-based QDs are carbon dots (CDs) which attracted enormous attention due to their higher biocompatibility and low toxicity [23-26]. Moreover, when compared to the semiconductors QDs, CDs display higher chemical inertness as well as a superior chemical stability, therefore being able to be used as reference fluorophores in ratiometric sensing approaches [27,28].

Among the different sensor arrangements that could be exploited by resorting to CIS or AIS QDs, the mixture of various QDs with dissimilar nature, size or composition, in a ratiometric probe with multi-emission, although not yet reported, may be foreseen as a valuable analytical strategy. Indeed, by using ratiometric sensing strategies, it is possible to circumvent detrimental variables not dependent on the analyte, usually observed in PL methodologies wherein the analytical readout depended on the modulation of the emission intensity of a single emitter sensing element. These includes excitation source oscillations, background fluorescence, complex matrix effects of samples, probe concentration and other environmental conditions related with interaction medium [28-33]. The readout of more than one emission intensities at distinct wavelengths allows to obtain a signal ratio that affords an intrinsic self-reference correction for signal variations and environmental effects. Consequently, an enhanced selectivity and a more accurate detection can be attained [30]. As examples, the use of dual emission probes, by blending CDs with semiconductors QDs, for the monitoring of different analytes in food [34], environmental [35-39], pharmaceutical [28,40] and biological samples [41-44], can be highlighted. It is worth mentioning that all abovementioned ratiometric platforms allowed a reliable and accurate determination, although concerns related to the toxicity of Cd-based QDs have been raised.

Additionally, QDs-based ratiometric sensing platforms can also be explored for visual detection approaches since they are able to engender diverse colours with characteristic tonalities. Effectively, in the presence of the analyte of interest, a wide colour tonality modulation may be perceived based on the changes in the emission intensities ratio. This enables the implementation of PL-based visual sensing platforms which can be employed as a rapid, on-site and at point-of-need screening assay without using sophisticated and expensive readout equipment [27].

In the present work, thiomalic acid (TMA)-capped AgInS2 QDs and CDs were combined as a ratiometric sensing platform for the fluorometric determination and visual monitoring of IBAN based on colour modulation. This nanoprobe consisted on the mixture of a carbon dot (blue PL emission), which is unreactive when interacting with IBAN, and a TMA-AIS QDs (orange PL emission) that is inhibited upon interaction with the bisphosphonate drug. The PL ratio and colour modulation of the sensing probe (orange to blue) enabled the quality control of IBAN in pharmaceutical formulations. The present work intended to demonstrate the use of Cd-free ternary AgInS2 QDs as valuable alternative regarding the most frequently used Cd-based QDs for the implementation of more environmentally friendly and less toxic ratiometric and visual analytical methodologies. To the best of our knowledge, ternary Cd-free QDs was used for the first time in visual sensing schemes.

The employed chemicals were of analytical grade without any modification or additional purification. The solutions and standard were prepared using ultrapure water with a conductivity ≤ 0.1 μS cm−1 from a Milli-Q system.

Two ibandronic acid stock solutions with concentration of 0.332 and 1.819 mmol L−1 were obtained by dissolving 10.6 and 58.1 mg of the bisphosphonate standard reagent (C9H23NO7P2, Sigma-Aldrich®, 95%, St. Louis, MO, USA) in 100 mL of deionized water to be used in ratiometric PL assays and visual detection approach, respectively.

For the preliminary assays, intermediate solutions of AIS QDs stabilized with TMA, D-Pen and MPA with concentrations of 1.14, 1.18 and 1.02 mg mL−1, respectively, were prepared through the dissolution of 5.7, 5.9 and 5.1 mg of the respective lyophilized powder in 5 mL of ultrapure water.

Three distinct pharmaceutical formulations, containing 150 mg of IBAN per tablet, commercially available in local pharmacies were used for the preparation of sample solutions. This was performed by powdering the unique tablet and dissolving the obtained powder in deionized water assisted through mechanical stirring for 30 min to guarantee an effective bisphosphonate drug extraction. Then, solutions were filtered into 250.0 mL volumetric flasks and completed with deionized water.

A CEM Discover SP® Microwave system containing an active cooling system (PowerMAXTM), an integrated infrared (IR) sensor and an automated pressure control/sensing system (ActiVentTM) were used for AgInS2 QDs synthesis. The rigorous control of temperature, pressure and irradiation time was conducted through Synergy™ software (Matthews, NC, US). The control of the pH for the synthetic process was accomplished using a pH-meter GLP 22 (CRISON). The separation of precipitated QDs was carried out by centrifugation using a refrigerated centrifuge (Thermo Electron Jouan BR4I, Waltham MA, USA). Additionally, a lyophilizer system (Telstar LyoQuest, Telstar Technologies, Spain) was employed for the lyophilization of the as-prepared AIS QDs solutions.

The quantum yields (QY) of all nanomaterials were measured at room temperature with an Absolute QY Spectrometer (Quantaurus QY—C11347-11, Hamamatsu, Japan). A Lifetime spectrofluorometer (DeltaFlexTM TCSPC, Horiba Scientific, Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a PPD picosecond detection module, NanoLED pulsed light source and DeltaHub timing electronics was used for the measurements of PL lifetime of all synthesized nanomaterials alone and by adding different amounts of analyte. A LUDOX® scattering solution was used as a lifetime reference, therefore allowing to calibrate the instrument response function (IRF) for the lifetime curve.
The PL emission spectra for the optical characterization of the nanoparticles and for the ratiometric PL assays were collected using a spectrofluorometer (Jasco FP-6500, Easton, MD, USA).

In the visual detection approach, a digital camera (Sony Cyber-shot DSC-HX10 V) were used for photographing the solutions in a dark room under irradiation of a 254 and 365 nm hand-held UV lamp (VL-6.LC, 6W).

ce:section.ce:section-title: AIS QDs and CDs synthesis procedure
ce:section.ce:para: For the semiconductors AgInS2 QDs synthesis, silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.9999%), ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 28.0–30.0% NH3 basis), mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, C3H6O2S, 99%) and thiomalic acid (TMA, C4H6O4S, ≥99.0%) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich® (St. Louis, MO, USA), while indium chloride (InCl3, 99.995%), sodium sulfide (Na2S·9H2O, 98+%) and D-penicillamine (D-Pen, C5H11NO2S, 99%) were obtained from Acros organics™ (New Jersey, USA).

The aqueous synthesis of AIS QDs were performed by using the microwave synthetic route following the protocol as described in the scientific literature for TMA [17], MPA [18] and D-Pen [45,46] capping agents. For TMA-capped AIS QDs, three distinctly emitting nanoparticles were synthesised, namely yellow (y-), orange (o-) and red (r-) with maximum emission wavelength at 576, 594 and 621 nm, respectively. These different emitting TMA-AIS QDs were obtained by changing the molar ratio of the precursors. All experimental conditions for each synthesized nanoparticle are summarized in Table S1 (supplementary material).

After the synthesis, the semiconductor ternary AIS QDs capped with TMA and D-Pen were directly lyophilized to remove the solvent. Therefore, the obtained powder can be stored for a longer period of time under standard laboratory conditions which could be redispersed at a required concentration in aqueous medium. As the AIS QDs capped with MPA had a broad emission band, absolute ethanol was used for size-selective precipitation allowing not only the removal of the excess of reactants but also to obtain a narrower emission band. Subsequently, the precipitates were separated through centrifugation for 10 min at 4000 rpm. Then, the obtained powder was re-suspended in deionized water and lyophilized as the other AIS QDs with distinct capping ligands. The obtained lyophilized powders were dried under vacuum and stored in amber vials from light for later use.

The synthesis of CDs was performed as our preceding work [28]. Briefly, 0.5 g of citric acid (Sigma–Aldrich®, St. Louis, MO, USA, C6H8O7, ≥99.5%) and 0.3 mL of ethylenediamine (Sigma-Aldrich®, St. Louis, MO, USA, N2C2H6, ≥99.5%) were mixed in deionized water obtaining a 10% (m/v) citric acid final concentration. Aiming at adjusting the pH of the mixture to 3.8, a required amount of 1 mol L−1 HCl solution was added. Then, the obtained solution was heated at 260 °C during 5 h in a 40 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. At the end of the synthesis reaction time, the as-prepared solution was allowed to cool at room temperature. Finally, the solution was dialyzed against Mili-Q water throughout 5 days, using a dialysis membrane (Spectra/Por 6–1000 MWCO, Spectrum Labs™).

For the implementation of ratiometric sensing platforms, the respective nanoparticle intermediate solutions were prepared previously. The CDs intermediate solution was obtained by directly diluting 100 µL of the crude solution in 150 mL of deionized water (ratio of 1:1500). The intermediate solution of AIS QDs stabilized with TMA was obtained by dissolving 5.7 mg of the lyophilized powder in 5 mL of deionized water obtaining a 1.14 mg mL−1 mass concentration.

The ratiometric assays were carried out by sequentially adding 20 µL of CDs intermediate solution and appropriate amounts of drug and deionized water into Eppendorf tubes. Then, 50 µL of 1.14 mg mL−1 TMA-AgInS2 QDs was added, making up the volume to 2 mL. After shaking, the solution was transferred to a quartz cell (10 mm of optical path). Then, by fixing the excitation wavelength at 360 nm, PL spectra (λem range from 380 to 700 nm) were obtained 5 min after the addition of AIS QDs. The slit widths for emission and excitation were set at 5.0 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for the visual detection approach
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of the visual ratiometric probe, an intermediate solution of CDs was obtained through the dilution of 200 µL of the crude solution in 20 mL of deionized water (dilution factor of 1:100). The semiconductor AIS QDs were used directly from the as-synthesized solution. The CDs/y-AIS QDs combined probe consisted in a mixture of 50 µL of CDs intermediate solution and 300 µL of y-AIS QDs crude solution for a 2 mL total volume. In the case of the CDs/o-AIS QDs arrangement, 50 µL and 400 µL of CDs intermediate solution and o-AIS QDs crude solution respectively, were mixed making up the volume to 2 mL with deionized water.

For the quantification of IBAN, 50 µL of CDs intermediate solution and appropriate amounts of ultrapure water and IBAN (standard or real sample solutions) were sequentially added. At the end, the pre-defined amount of AIS QDs (300 of y-AIS QDs or 400 µL of o-AIS QDs crude solution) was added making up a total volume of 2 mL. After shaking, the resulting solutions were transferred to a quartz cell with 10 mm of optical path and one minute later were photographed with a digital camera in a dark room under irradiation at 365 nm with a UV lamp. The obtained photographs were cropped to 170 × 280 pixels dimension image and then converted into matrices with 170 rows and 280 columns. Each photograph generated three matrices, corresponding to the intensity values (between 0 and 255) of red, green and blue channels. Then, the mean value of each colour was calculated using a central area of each photograph, which contained a rectangle with a dimension of 50 X 100 pixels. Therefore, for each photograph three values (one value for each colour) were obtained. At the end, blue and red channels intensities ratio were calculated and further used to assess the concentration of IBAN in standards and samples solutions. The conversion of the photographs to matrices and the respective calculation of the mean values of each colour was performed using Matab version R2014a (MathWorks, Natick, USA).

The synthesized nanomaterials were characterized by steady-state and time-resolved fluorimetry aiming at evaluating their optical properties. The emission spectra and quantum yields are relevant information for the development of an efficient ratiometric probe for both fluorometric and visual detection. On the other hand, the measurement of the fluorescence lifetime values is important to understand the quenching mechanism of the QDs upon interacting with the analyte.

The quantum yields values of all nanomaterials were measured being summarized in Table 1. It is worth mentioning that the QY values for all the ternary QDs decreased upon the lyophilization process in which an inhibition of 17.7, 59.6, 41.8% was observed for TMA-AIS, D-Pen-AIS and MPA-AIS QDs, respectively.

The PL dynamic properties of semiconductor QDs and CDs were also evaluated to determine the radiative and non-radiative relaxation rates (Table 2 ). For each nanomaterial, the obtained curves (Fig. S1 – supplementary material) revealed multiexponential emission decays wherein a three-component exponential-decay function was used to fit the obtained data (Fig. S1 – supplementary material). Therefore, no significant deviation of the residuals and low values of chi-squared was obtained. The results revealed that the fluorescence emission for all the synthesized nanomaterials occur by three different radiative processes (Table 2). This corresponds to a short (τ values ranging 0.59–4.4 ns for AIS QDs and 1.25 ns for CDs), medium (τ values ranging 25.1–39.7 ns for AIS QDs and 4.41 ns for CDs) and long (τ values ranging 218.6–267.5 ns for AIS QDs and 10.38 ns for CDs) decay component. For the whole decay process, the average lifetime was calculated as
τ
average
=
∑ i
B i
τ
i
2
∑ i
B i
τ i
, in which Bi signifies the amplitude of each decay fraction and τi is the corresponding time constant. The obtained average lifetime depicted in Table 2 clearly demonstrates that not only the surface capping ligand but also the composition of the nanomaterial plays a crucial role in the PL lifetime.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preliminary assays for conventional fluorometric assay
ce:section.ce:para: The modification of the QDs reactivity can be achieved by selecting an appropriate short-chained thiol-based ligands as stabilizing capping agent. Effectively, the presence of dissimilar terminal functional moieties affects not only the nanoparticle surface charge, but also the QDs sensitivity and selectivity towards the analyte of interest [15,32,33]. Aiming at developing a ratiometric sensing platform for IBAN, the individual interaction between the bisphosphonate drug with CDs and three distinct AIS QDs capped with distinct capping ligands, namely TMA, MPA and D-penicillamine (D-Pen), was carefully evaluated.

Before the interaction study with IBAN, the concentrations of all nanoparticle probes were adjusted to obtain an identical emission intensity for each as-prepared QDs. Considering the different quantum yields of the as-prepared AIS QDs, concentrations of 28.5, 59.0 and 25.5 µg mL−1 for TMA, D-Pen and MPA-stabilized AIS QDs, respectively, were used for the interaction study with IBAN at concentration values of up to 303.54 µmol L−1. Regarding the CDs, a 1:150000 dilution ratio from the synthesized solution was tested for the interaction with IBAN.

By analysing the results (Fig. 2 and Fig. S2 – supplementary material), a PL emission quenching of all ternary QDs was observed upon interaction with IBAN. However, the magnitude of the QDs PL quenching, when interacting with the bisphosphonate drug, was dependent on the chemical nature of the capping ligands attached to the QDs surface.

Indeed, the quenching effect on TMA-AIS QDs (Fig. 2a) was much more sensitive than that observed when using MPA-AIS (Fig. 2b) and D-Pen-AIS QDs (Fig. 2c), wherein only minor variations on the PL intensity were observed. Through the analysis of the linear Stern-Volmer plots, related with the PL response of the three distinctly capped AIS QDs when interacting with IBAN, a superior sensitivity was confirmed by applying TMA-AIS QDs as sensing element (Fig. S2 – supplementary material). Effectively, the obtained Stern-Volmer quenching constant when using TMA-AIS QDs (Ksv = 0.026 ± 0.002 L µmol−1) was 37 and 54 times superior to that observed by applying D-Pen-AIS QDs (Ksv = 0.0007 ± 0.0001 L µmol−1) and MPA-AIS QDs (Ksv = 0.00048 ± 0.00005 L µmol−1), respectively, as PL probe.

When using CDs, their emission intensity did not change significantly after the interaction with IBAN at concentration values of up to 303.54 µmol L −1 (Fig. 2d). In fact, when CDs are used without any surface functionalization, they are highly chemically inert towards the presence of a given specie comparatively to the high reactivity exhibited by binary and/or ternary semiconductors QDs (such as CdTe and AgInS2 QDs). In addition, they exhibit exceptional chemical stability [27,28].

PL lifetime of 28.5 µg mL−1 TMA-AIS QDs solutions in the absence and by adding IBAN with increasing concentrations (up to 100.55 µmol L−1) were measured to clarify whether the PL quenching process was dynamic or static. The obtained results (Fig. S3 and Fig. S4) revealed that the PL lifetime of TMA-AIS QDs remains practically unaffected by adding increasing concentrations of IBAN. This could be explained by the occurrence of quenching mechanisms relying on static processes, which yielded a non-fluorescent ground-state complex between the PL probe and the PL inhibiting specie [47]. Indeed, when static quenching takes place, it only yields non-fluorescent complexed fluorophores while the properties of the fluorescent species that are not complexed remain unchanged [47].

Therefore, the obtained results complied with the PL quenching mechanism proposed in scientific literature [14] wherein the inhibition of the PL properties of semiconductor quantum dots was ascribed to photoinduced electron transfer (PET) processes. Under this mechanism a complex was yielded between the electron donor and the acceptor [48]. Then, the excited fluorophore (QDs) can be either the electron donor or acceptor depending on the oxidation and reduction potential of ground and excited states [48]. In this particular, the interactions between the tertiary amine moiety of IBAN and the incomplete coordination bonds at QDs surface prevents electron-hole recombination which results on the inhibition of the nanoparticle photoluminescence. Despite being less reactive than primary and secondary ones, tertiary amines can act as electron donor agents [49,50], and thus electron transfer from the amine moiety of IBAN to the photogenerated hole of AIS QDs can be anticipated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs/AIS-QDs ratiometric sensing platform for IBAN detection
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the distinct reactivities of each nanoparticle tested, a ratiometric probe composed by an analyte-dependent fluorophore (ADF: PL properties are modulated when interacting with the analyte) and a reference fluorophore (RF: PL properties remained unchanged when interacting with the analyte) was assembled through the mixture of AIS QDs stabilized with TMA as ADF and CDs as RF.

A ratiometric probe with similar initial intensities of the two emission bands was obtained by mixing 20 μL of the intermediate CDs solution (1500 × ) and 50 μL of the AIS QDs with a concentration of 1.14 mg mL−1, for a total volume of 2 mL.

The PL spectra of CDs (blue emission) and TMA-AIS QDs (orange emission) as well as the obtained ratiometric probe are represented in Fig. S5. As can be perceived, upon the combination of CDs with TMA-AIS QDs, the emission intensity of both nanomaterials was inhibited by approximately 50% relatively to that observed for each nanoparticle individually. This can be ascribed to the fact that both nanoparticles share the same excitation light source wherein their excitation bands are overlapping which can lead to an inner filter effect between each other.
Additionally, a blue shift of the maximum emission wavelength of TMA-AIS QDs (592 to 583 nm) was verified while the maximum emission corresponding to the CDs remains at 430 nm.

As the accuracy and reproducibility of consecutive PL measurements could be affected by the nanoprobe stability, the PL intensity of the 430/583 nm ratiometric sensing platform (FICD/FIAIS) alone and by adding 16.60 µmol L−1 of IBAN were monitored throughout 30 min (Fig. 3 ).

The obtained results showed that in the absence of IBAN, fluorescence intensity ratios (FICD/FIAIS) remained practically constant up to 10 min and thereafter increased for longer periods of time, confirming that the ratiometric probe was stable in aqueous medium for the first 10 min.

With respect to the kinetics of the reaction between the combined probe and IBAN, the analytical signal (FI ratio) increased gradually up to 5 min, remaining practically unchanged for longer times. This demonstrated that the reaction occurred promptly and throughout 5 min. In this sense, bearing in mind the obtained results and aiming at ensuring a better compromise between reactivity and stability for the subsequent assays, the PL response of the probe was recorded 5 min after the interaction with the analyte.
The PL properties of the dual-emission (430/583 nm) probe were evaluated for IBAN concentrations up to 307.15 µmol L−1.

As predicted, the two typical emission bands exhibited dissimilar responses in the presence of the analyte. In fact, the PL intensity of the AIS QDs emission band at 583 nm was successively inhibited by adding increasing concentrations of bisphosphonate, while a slight increase of the PL intensity of CDs at 430 nm was observed (Fig. 4 a). It is worth mentioning that the slight increase of CDs PL emission mostly resulted from the quenching effect of IBAN on the optical properties of AIS QDs. As observed in preliminary assays, CDs are chemically inert to the presence of IBAN. Effectively, with the inhibition of the PL properties of AIS QDs caused by the interaction with the bisphosphonate drug, the possible inner filter effect between both nanoparticles was impaired and consequently the optical properties of CDs were partially recovered. These results endorse the valuable combination of both nanomaterials for IBAN detection in which TMA-AgInS2 QDs are employed as the recognition fluorophore while the CDs emission band affords an intrinsic self-reference correction for signal variations and environmental effects.

The analytical performance of the dual-emission probe was confirmed by evaluating the limit of detection (LOD), linear working range, accuracy and precision. Under optimized experimental conditions, a relationship between the emission intensity ratio (FICD/FIAIS) and the concentration of IBAN of up to 33.20 µmol L−1 was linear (Fig. 4b). The obtained regression equation can be expressed as:

A LOD of 3.07 µmol L−1 (0.99 mg L−1) was obtained and it was calculated considering the equation of the calibration curve [51]. The obtained LOD was much lower than the declared content of IBAN in pharmaceutical formulations (150 mg per tablet).

Aiming at demonstrating the advantage of ratiometric determination regarding the conventional single wavelength method, the interaction of the single emitter AIS QDs and the ratiometric probe with increasing concentrations of IBAN (up to 33.20 µmol L−1) was evaluated. The obtained calibration curves demonstrated that by using the ratiometric probe, an improved linear regression was observed with a correlation coefficient of 0.9964 while for the single emitter AIS QDs the obtained value was 0.9814 (Fig S6 - supplementary material). Additionally, the obtained LOD when using the ratiometric probe (3.07 µmol L−1/0.99 mg L−1) was about twice as small as the one obtained when using a single emitter AIS QDs (6.67 µmol L−1/2.15 mg L−1).

Before the applicability in pharmaceutical samples, the influence in the IBAN monitoring of some possible interfering species usually present in the pharmaceutical formulation was thoroughly assessed. Thus, solutions with 16.60 µmol L−1 of IBAN in the absence and presence of diverse amounts of interfering species, such as polyvinylpyrrolidone, lactose monohydrate, hydroxypropyl methylcellulose and polyethylene glycol, were evaluated. Through the analysis of the obtained results, variations lower than ± 5% were verified between the ratiometric PL signals for IBAN solutions in the absence and by adding water-soluble excipients with mass quantities close to that found in real samples.

The accuracy of the ratiometric PL methodology was assessed through the quantification of IBAN in three different commercially available pharmaceutical formulations and by carrying out recovery assays wherein each one was spiked with two distinct concentrations (4.98 and 8.30 µmol L −1) of IBAN standard solution. The results compiled in Table 3 shown that the amount of IBAN found for all the tested samples was in compliance with the declared ones. The intramethod precision was evaluated by carrying out 3 repeated determinations of each sample solution. In view of the obtained concentration ranges at 95% confidence level, one can conclude that the proposed method revealed a good repeatability.
The recovery and the relative standard deviation (RSD) values of each IBAN concentration spiked, which were carried out in triplicate, are also shown in Table 3.

The recovery and RSD values ranged from 98 to 103% and from 1.49 to 2.15%, respectively, which indicates that the existing excipients in the pharmaceutical samples did not interfere significantly in the proposed sensing platform for IBAN detection.
This fact demonstrates that the proposed ratiometric probe could be applied for the determination of IBAN in pharmaceutical samples with high reliability, selectivity and accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing platform for visual detection of IBAN
ce:section.ce:para: Parallel to the ratiometric PL measurements, the dual-emission probe can be explored in visual sensing to implement valuable analytical tools for on-site and at point-of-need screening of target analytes without resorting to sophisticated readout equipment. Therefore, considering the reactivity of the aforementioned nanomaterials, three distinct QDs-based ratiometric sensing platforms were tested, involving the combination of blue-emitting CDs (λem = 430 nm) with three distinct TMA-AIS QDs emitting at different wavelengths and consequently with distinct colour tonality emissions (Fig. 5 ). Thus, yellow (y-), orange (o-) and red (r-) emitting TMA-AIS QDs with maximum emission wavelengths at 576, 594, 621 nm, respectively, were evaluated for the development of the visual sensing probe.

As the sensitivity of the ratiometric visual probe depends on the capacity of the human eye to discriminate the colour tonality, a rigorous and careful optimization study of the concentration of the sensing platform was performed. Effectively, the probe concentration should be adequate to be perceptible to naked eye without impairing the LOD of the analytical approach. Accordingly, depending on the QY of QDs, high nanocrystals concentrations must be used aiming the obtaining of an analyte determination with high accuracy. For the development of the visual probe, the nanomaterials were used directly from the crude solution wherein the QY of CDs, y-, o-, and red- TMA-AIS QDs were 20.8(±0.2), 14.9(±0.3), 12.4(±0.3) and 4.9(±0.1)%, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 5, diverse concentrations levels of each nanomaterial have to be used to obtain a sensitive colour intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization study for the development of the CDs/AIS QDs arrangement
ce:section.ce:para: The ensuing assays focused on the optimization of the visual ratiometric probe for IBAN monitoring in pharmaceutical formulations. Thus, three distinct arrangements were studied by fixing the dilution factor of CDs at 1:4000 and by adjusting the concentration level of the ternary QDs using different dilution factor (Fig. 6 ). The higher dilution factor tested for CDs resulted from the fact that, acting as reference fluorophore in the visual ratiometric probe, this concentration level was sufficient to be perceived by naked eye.

Through the analysis of the obtained images, only the combinations of CDs/y-AIS QDs and CDs/o-AIS QDs provided a valuable visual sensing of IBAN (Fig. 6a and 6b). In fact, the obtained colour, that results from the combination between the two PL probes, has a characteristic tonality which enables the detection of the analyte through the gradual modulation of the colour tonality upon the interaction process. The selected dilution factor permitted an improved compromise between LOD and sensitivity.

On the other hand, the combination of CDs/r-AIS QDs was not feasible since even for higher concentrations of the ternary QDs, the colour tonality of the mixture was similar to the characteristic colour of the reference fluorophore (CDs). For this reason, the combinations of CDs/y-AIS and CDs/o-AIS QDs were chosen for the following assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability study of the visual sensing probes
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of CDs/y-AIS and CDs/o-AIS arrangements alone and after interaction with IBAN, at a concentration range up to 1.33 mmol L−1, was thoroughly studied by naked eye monitoring of the colour tonality modulation during 5 min. For assembling the visual ratiometric probes, the CDs dilution factor was fixed at 1:4000, while the dilution factors used for y-AIS QDs and o-AIS QDs were 1:6.7 and 1:5, respectively. These conditions were selected because at these concentration levels it was possible to obtain not only a sensitive visual detection but also an adequate reactivity towards the analyte.

The obtained images demonstrated that the colour of both combined probes alone and after interaction with the analyte remained stable for the first 5 min at the concentrations tested, indicating that the colour readout can be performed within this period of time (Figs. S7 and S8 – supplementary material). Additionally, the ratiometric sensing platform composed by CDs/o-AIS QDs can generate a wider and more profuse colour tonality evolution upon interaction with the target analyte when compared to that observed using CDs/y-AIS QDs arrangement. This effect on the colour tonality modulation is more effortlessly recognised by the human eye therefore allowing the analyte quantification with higher reliability and accuracy.

For quantification purposes, the CDs/o-AIS QDs system was used as visual probe since it allowed the obtaining of a more sensitive visual readout. The colour images of the visual sensing platform in absence and presence of IBAN standard solutions with a known concentration (between 0.03 and 1.41 mmol L−1) and real samples were acquired with a digital camera under irradiation at 365 nm by using a conventional UV lamp (Fig. 7 a.). Each acquired image was converted on its RGB values, as previously described (section 2.5.) and the blue and red channels intensities ratio were used for concentration assessment.

By plotting the blue and red channels intensities ratio versus IBAN concentration (Fig. 7b.), two linear relationships were observed, the first for concentrations values ranging from 0.03 to 0.89 mmol L−1 (
I
blue
I
red
= 0.23
± 0.02
×
I B A N
+ 1.023
( ± 0.008 )
, R = 0.9807, n = 10) and a second one between 0.97 and 1.33 mmol L−1 (
I
blue
I
red
= 2.0
± 0.2
×
I B A N
- 0.6
( ± 0.2 )
, R = 0.9825 , n = 6 )
.

The latter linear regression was more suitable for the quantification of IBAN in pharmaceutical formulation because it provided a 10-fold higher sensitivity regarding the former one. The obtained results (Table S3 – supplementary material) demonstrated a good agreement between the PL ratiometric method and the RGB-visual assay regarding the obtained relative deviations.

In this work, a ratiometric sensing platform with dual-emission which combined CDs (blue emission) and TMA-AIS QDs (orange emission) was efficaciously implemented in the detection of IBAN in pharmaceutical samples. The addition of IBAN induced the inhibition of the emission intensity of TMA-AIS QDs (ADF) whilst the luminescence of CDs remained practically unchanged due to its chemical inertness towards the antiresorptive drug (RF). The determination in pharmaceutical formulations revealed that the amount of IBAN found for all tested samples was in accordance to the reported, with recovery values ranging from 98 to 103%.

Furthermore, in order to develop a screening test which allowed a point-of-need screening of target analyte without the use of sophisticated instrumentation, a visual sensing approach was proposed. The option for a ratiometric sensing platform instead of single emitter sensing element was justified by the fact that the former allows to monitor IBAN throughout the modulation of the colour tonality while the latter only enables the visualization of the target analyte throughout the modulation of colour brightness. Considering that the human eye is more sensitive to tonality variation rather than to brightness modulation, a more sensitive visual detection is obtained by using a ratiometric probe.

After a detailed and careful optimization process, the CDs/o-AIS QDs system was used as visual probe since it allowed a wide colour variation. In fact, upon the interaction of IBAN, the tonality colour of the solution progressively modifies from a specific colour (resulting from the two nanoparticles mixture) towards the characteristic blue colour of the CDs (RF). To date, and according to our knowledge, ternary Cd-free QDs was used for the first time in visual sensing schemes. Ternary Cd-free QDs, as AIS QDs, can be efficaciously used instead of the Cd-based QDs because they are less toxic and more environmentally friendly nanoparticles. In fact, AIS QDs allow the elimination of hazardous elements in the composition of QDs, such as cadmium. However, as ternary QDs displayed a characteristic broad emission band, the obtained colour were not so well defined comparatively to that observed with Cd-based QDs with binary arrangements, which usually exhibit much narrower emission bands. This characteristic makes more difficult to implement visual platforms using this type of nanomaterials. Despite this, in this work, the combination of CDs with AIS QDS showed that the visual sensing approach can be implemented efficaciously for rapid screening of IBAN in pharmaceutical formulations.

Rafael C. Castro: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Visualization. Ricardo N.M.J. Páscoa: Visualization, Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. M. Lúcia M.F.S. Saraiva: Validation, Funding acquisition, Supervision. João L.M. Santos: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. David S.M. Ribeiro: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120592.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
